chunks.embed_text: Can a player take 2 gems of one color if there are at least 4 tokens of that color left when the player takes them, or does it mean there must be at least 4 tokens left after taking them?
chunks.content: The community resolved that a player can take 2 gems of one color if there are at least 4 tokens of that color present when the player takes them, not after. This interpretation is supported by the explanation that 'when the player takes them' refers to the moment of taking, not after.

raw_thread.body:
the rules say "This action is only possible if there are at least 4 tokens of the chosen color left when the player takes them. I have already been taught two different ways. One review said this means you cannot take 2 if there are 4 in the start because the left means after you take them. Another player told me you can take them as long as there are 4 left when you take them. 

Please help: Which is correct?
It means 4 left before you take them. It's clearer in some of the other languages and that's also how the publisher describes it in a video on Tric Trac.

Thanks for the quick reply. A Rules editor of mine told me the word "Left" means that it means you cannot take them if less than 4 are left after your take. I thought your answer made more sense so that in a two player game you at least have one shot at a full stack to get 2.

"When the player takes them" ≠ "after the player takes them".

"When the player takes them" = "at the moment that the player takes them".

W Eric Thanks for the quick reply. A Rules editor of mine told me the word "Left" means that it means you cannot take them if less than 4 are left after your take.

"When the player takes them" ≠ "after the player takes them".

"When the player takes them" = "at the moment that the player takes them".

Yeah, right !

Regards,

CROC

If you couldn't take two gems of a color unless there will be 4 left after you take them, then this option would be available only in the 4-player game!

With 2-3 players, you start with only 4-5 games per color - taking 2 of those would always leave less than 4!

With 2-3 players, you start with only 4-5 games per color - taking 2 of those would always leave less than 4!

So that means, in a 2p game if there are 4 gems/tokens of a certain color, it is allowed to take 2 gems/tokens of that color. In other words: The pair-picking action is allowed. Then 2 tokens of that color are left on the table. The pair-picking action is not allowed anymore (for that color), until at least 4 tokens of that color come back to the table (through payments).
Woo!  The 9 year clarification!

chunks.thread_subject: Splendor 10 chip limit Returning chips question
chunks.embed_text: In Splendor, if a player's chip count exceeds 10 at the end of their turn, can they return any chips to reach the limit, or are they limited to returning chips they just drew? Can a player choose to return chips from their existing pile instead of the newly drawn chips?
chunks.content: The community consensus is that a player can return any chips to reach the 10 chip limit, not just the chips they just drew. The rule allows for returning all or some of the chips drawn, but this does not constrain the player to only return those chips. The player can choose to return any combination of chips from their total pile to get back to the 10 chip limit.

raw_thread.body:
Hi,

Have only played the game twice so far but wanted to clariy a rule.

A player may not have over 10 chips in their hand at the end of their turn.

They must return down to 10 tokens if they are over the limit by the end of their turn.

The rules state that: "A player can return all or some of those (chips) they've just drawn."

So just wanted to clarify that if at the beginning of your turn you have either 8, 9, or 10 chips in your area, you are still able to choose to pick up 3 different coloured chips (which brings your total to either 11, 12 or 13 chips), as long as you return chips until you reach 10 chips again by the end of your turn? Is that correct?

Also, when returning chips down to 10 if you're over the limit you can return all or some of the chips that you've just picked up (that brought you over the 10 chip limit). 

But can you return for example, none of the 3

different coloured chips that you just picked up this turn and choose to return 3 other combinations of chips (eg 3 other different coloured chips or 3 of the same coloured chips or other combo) from your pile of chips as an option?

Thanks!

Alan - you just need to look at the rule in its simplest terms.  

You can always take three tokens (or less depending on the action).  However, after doing so, you need to check if you have more than ten.  If so, return any chips so that you're back down to the limit.  Don't worry about the colours, just focus on the ten chip limit.

Hope this helps.

Right.

You can return any tokens you want to get down to 10.

The statement "A player can return all or some of those they've just drawn," is there just to emphasize that your are not constrained to returning tokens you had at the beginning of the turn.

In particular, the following is allowed:  you start a turn with 9 tokens, take one each of Emerald, Sapphire, and Ruby, and then return the Sapphire and Ruby.

Maybe you really needed an Emerald - and wanted to keep the 9 tokens with which you started the turn.  This is a legal way to get that Emerald.

Great thanks Rompa and gillum for confirming and clarifying!

This helped!

I had the same question. The answer was what I expected, but the English rules do not convey clearly the intent that has been communicated here. The mention of the gems that you have just taken comes across as a logical constraint, in my reading, at least. I suspect many less experienced gamers, which seems to be the target audience, will play the rule incorrectly.

I hope that the publisher takes this feedback to heart and adds another simple sentence to the next printing of the rules that explicitly states the rule, something like, "any gems in your possession may returned to reach the limit of 10." Seriously, the rule as currently written does not logically imply this to be the case, because only one use case is provided along with the limit of 10 rule.

Nice game, by the way!

How can you take a total of 13 the the rules clearly state… The rules state that: "A player can return all or some of those (chips) they've just drawn.".      JUST DRAWN…so there is no exchanging chips to get back to 10. 

I'm just saying🤔

I'm just saying🤔

The intention of that text is literally that you CAN return those you just drew, but you're NOT FORCED to return those. (As gillum wrote upthread, "The statement "A player can return all or some of those they've just drawn," is there just to emphasize that your are not constrained to returning tokens you had at the beginning of the turn.")

The full rule is:
A player can never have more than 10 tokens at the end of their turn (including jokers). If this happens, they must return tokens until they only have 10 left. A player can return all or some of those they've just drawn.

Yes, it could have been phrased better. But the intent is clear and established.

A player can never have more than 10 tokens at the end of their turn (including jokers). If this happens, they must return tokens until they only have 10 left. A player can return all or some of those they've just drawn.

So if you have 10 tokens, and you draw 3, then at the end of your turn, the pool from which you must return tokens is all 13 tokens.

The clarification about "A player can return..." is because otherwise it could have been argued that they would take 3, and they had to get rid of 3 of their existing pot of tokens to make room for the 3 they just picked up.

This rule basically says "The tokens you pick up are instantly part of your token pot."  That way the "They must return tokens..." statement applies to them too.

chunks.embed_text: What are the additional tie-breakers for the game Splendor when the official rule of fewest development cards is not enough to determine a winner?
chunks.content: The community suggests several tie-breakers, including the player owning the most nobles tiles, the player owning the most gems, and the last player in the order of play. Mr Poulpe, a representative of Space Cowboys, provided an unofficial but authoritative answer: the player with the most nobles tiles, then the player owning the most gems, and finally the last player in the order of play. This order is also followed in the official tournament rules for the online app.

raw_thread.body:
Just played a game where tied on points and number of development cards. We chose to use level 3 cards as second tie breaker (and level 2, then 1, if necessary).

Thoughts?
Would the winner be the player with the least amount of level 3 cards?

Sorry, didn't mean to leave that out. We chose who had the most because the level 3 ones take the most work to get to, and it seemed in the spirit of the first tie breaker: who got the most points with the fewest cards.

Thoughts?

I love the notion of deciding tie-breaker criteria in the aftermath of a tie.

What's wrong with a draw anyway?
Seems to me that this is a game where you could just keep going until there's a clear winner.

Thoughts?

Potentially you could decide based on # of nobles, although I wouldn't want players to feel pushed towards focusing on nobles as a strategy.

You could decide based on # of gems in hand, although you would need to decide how many gems a gold token is worth. Based on the actions, a gold token is worth more than one gem (since it's wild) but less than three gems (assuming all actions are meant to be equivalent, because you also reserve a card when you get a gold). So, two?

You could decide based on who has the single most valuable card. And should there be a tie for 5-point cards you could then proceed to 4, 3, etc. as needed. However, this might also push players towards a certain strategy.

I sort of like the idea of just continuing the game, although this could allow someone who has not initially reached 15 at the "conclusion" of the game to then win when it is continued (which would be dramatic, but it doesn't feel right).

Declaring a draw also seems ok, but not ideal.

I really hope the designer or publisher weighs in.

I've asked the author about tie-breakers. 

Please note this is not official as of now, but you may use it if you need to determine a winner (in tournaments, for instance) :

So, in order of priority :

- The player with the less number of development cards (already there, and official)

- The player owning the most nobles tiles

- The player owning the most gems (chips)

- If you're still tied (highly unlikely !) : the last player in the order of play.
That'll be no more sleepless nights for me.

Seems like you could also use the the number of reserved unpurchased development cards (in hand).

That is, ties would be broken in favor of the player holding the fewest such cards.

One could argue that a player who reached the same total points while holding the fewest reserved cards did so based more on skill than on stealing cards away from other players.

(Of course, you could use the same argument for breaking ties in favor of the player with the least gems held, not the most!)

Would the winner be the player with the least amount of level 3 cards?

Sorry, didn't mean to leave that out. We chose who had the most because the level 3 ones take the most work to get to, and it seemed in the spirit of the first tie breaker: who got the most points with the fewest cards.

This is an interesting notion, but it seems a little fiddly.  That is, it seems like the players involved with the tie would likely have an equal number of level 3 cards.  Did someone have more level 3 cards in your game?  

Someone may have more level 3 cards if another player has more nobles, which is another reason I do not like level 3 cards as a tie breaker.  Most nobles as a tie breaker seems to punish players for not choosing the noble strategy, and this is not a tendency I like in the game because diverse strategies make for interesting and fun game-play.    

I played a game where there was a tie on points and cards.  I suggested using most chips as tie breaker because it can be seen as a marker of efficiency, which is why I believe fewest cards is a tie breaker.  

This is another good option that I did not think of.  Players could continue for another round until the tie is broken.  This could add some additional tension to the game because someone may potentially pull ahead of the tied players.  There have been games where I could have made huge score differences if I simply had one more turn.  This could definitely be interesting.

That is, ties would be broken in favor of the player holding the fewest such cards.

One could argue that a player who reached the same total points while holding the fewest reserved cards did so based more on skill than on stealing cards away from other players.

(Of course, you could use the same argument for breaking ties in favor of the player with the least gems held, not the most!)

Using gems as a tie breaker is tricky because of certain assumptions.  I argued for least gems because it could be a sign that a player had built an efficient economy to where he/she did not need to take gems.  That is, he/she used development cards instead of gems to purchase more development cards.  However, a player may have used all or most of his/her gems to purchase a development card and break the tie even though he/she was not as efficient as the other player.

Admittedly, using most gems is probably not the best tie breaker either.
Thanks for this. We had a tie in our second ever game, decided by the nobles tiles.

That is, ties would be broken in favor of the player holding the fewest such cards.

I second this.  This idea seems almost part-and-parcel of the current official "fewest development cards wins" tie-breaker.

So I'd favor the following tie-breaker schedule:

- The player with the fewest development cards 

- The player currently holding the fewest reserve cards

- The player owning the most nobles tiles

- The player owning the most gems (chips)

- If you're still tied (highly unlikely !) : the last player in the order of play.

I was initially drawn to the idea of "if tied, play another round."  If Splendor were just a two player game, then I'd likely favor that.  But the extra round can get awkward with more than two players.  

For instance, if all players play an extra round (not just the players tied for the win) then you could get some awkward cases such as the following:  P1 has 15 points, P2 has 15 points, P3 has 14 points.  Another round is played in which P1 gets 1 more point, P2 gets 2 more points, and P3 gets 4 more points.  So after the extra round, the scores are P1 = 16, P2 = 17, P3 = 18.  P3 wins the game, even though P3 was the last player to cross the 15 point victory line.  That doesn't seem right!

Alternatively, one could say that only the tied players play another round (and potentially, yet another round, and another, until the tie is broken).  But that amounts to adding player elimination into Splendor, making it not my cup of tea (I'm not a fan of player elimination).

So I prefer the schedule of tie-breakers listed above.

Hey Mr Poulpe! 

I'm a member of a board game league in my town and we play games competitively that are scored throughout the course of a season (two months). One issue we ran into last night was additional tie breakers for the game Splendor.

We both had the same number of development cards (9), the same number of nobles tiles (0) and the same number of chips (0). So I guess that my opponent won because I was the starting player.

Before I found this forum thread I was really upset and felt it was very unfair that I didn't win the game.
I had used less thinking time than the other player, and since that's what determines who wins when a game is abandoned, I felt that this should have been the tiebreaker.

(When in a game that's abandoned you get the following message:
Game abandoned

chunks.embed_text: Are reserved cards in Splendor hidden from other players?
chunks.content: The community is divided on whether reserved cards are hidden or not. Some players believe that all reserved cards should be hidden, while others think that face-up cards should remain visible. There is no official ruling on this issue, but many players and digital implementations of the game treat reserved cards as hidden information.

raw_thread.body: From memory, the rule book is explicit about reserve cards drawn face down from the piles staying face down in your hand. Is it implied (or explicit somewhere) that cards reserved from the face-up cards become hidden in your hand? (I played a friend's copy tonight and don't have the rules in front of me now.)
Cards you reserve are always hidden. They go right to your hand. Cards in hand are never shown.
People actually hold cards in splendor? We never do.

yep they are hidden.

You can also take a the top face down card on one of the stacks and don't need to show it to other players.
True. We just place them face down and hope our opponents forget about them so we can make a game winning move with them when they least expect it.

We put all cards purchased on the table in front of us as well in groups. I can't imagine trying to keep them all in my hand.  Plus if you put the reserved card in your hand, how does anyone know when you actually pay for it?  Guess I need to start looking at images of Splendor to see what others are doing...
'In hand' is a conceptual term denoting cards that are treated differently than the ones kept face up on the table. They needn't be literally in your hand; face down on the table is fine.
we tap our reserved cards until they're paid for, face up, too busy concentrating on our own cards to memorize opponents reserved hand.

The reserve cards being discussed are the ones you take when you take the action to reserve a card to your hand (hand limit 3) and take a gold token; not when you purchase a card to add to your rows.  =)
Yes indeed the reserved cards are hidden.  You can take one from the board or from the op of a deck, place them in your hand (which is hidden from other players) and then take a Gold.

Why not?

IMHO, this is a huge part of the strategy, as you can use it to block, guarantee that said card will be yours, or simply add a gold and a card - which increases flexibility later.  

There was actually a game where I reserved a card (which I intended to build) but never even built it.  A different card came up later (worth more VP) - and the gold taken gave me the flexibility to build the "better version".

Several times it has been done when multiple players are vying for the same noble - and all need "just one more X card" - so a player reserves it, even if they can't build it, hoping that another card of the same type doesn't turn up. 

We've even had a game where someone reserved a card "blind" - off the draw pile - as they didn't like the options on the table.

Reserving cards is a very useful strategy - especially in 4 player games.  

But in answer to the OP - we always play face down.

After reserving a card and getting a gold token, when should I buy the reserved card? Can that card remain reserved all throughout the game? Do you lose if that card cannot be bought during the course of the game?

When you "should" buy it depends entirely on what your plan for that card is.  If you grabbed a level 3 one to work towards then build it when you can.  Was it to stop someone else from grabbing it, or simply to get the gold for later on...too many options to tell you an optimal timing.

The cards you reserve remain in your hand indefinitely, but you're only allowed 3 of them and the only way to remove one from your hand to make space for another is to play (buy/build) it.  

There's no penalty for hands left in reserve at the end of the game.

@TheOneEared Thank you!  There was this time I already had 20 points but I was waiting to get green gems(emeralds) to buy/build my reserved card in  order to win. So a player can reserve 3 cards and not buy/build any?  Also you can buy/build the reserved card anytime during the course of the game?  No need to buy it on the next turn after reserving it? 

Anytime someone has 15 points worth of cards/personalities then the last turn is triggered. If it was the starting player who reached 15 points then everyone else has 1 more turn. If it was the last turn who triggered the end game then the game ends immediately. Everyone must have had the same amount of turns by game end.

You are not required to build a reserved card in order to win a game. As long as you have the most points when the end game is triggered then you win.

So a player can reserve 3 cards and not buy/build any?  Also you can buy/build the reserved card anytime during the course of the game?  No need to buy it on the next turn after reserving it? 

My pleasure! 

A player can indeed reserve 3 cards they have no intention of building; it'll give you 3 Gold and could mess up someone else's plans.  There's no "deadline" on when you need to build them, nor is it a condition to end the game; it ends (with a final turn) when someone hits 15 points.   

You can reserve and build from your reserve as one of your normal actions, so you'd do it instead of taking gems etc.

Taking a card that someone else wants is probably the most aggressive thing you do here, so I guess it's up to your personal playstyle how you handle it and how fair it is.

Is there any official ruling on this?

The rules say that cards that are reserved from the face-down deck are not shown to other players, but it doesn't say anything about the reserved face-up cards. So we figured that since the reserved card was face-up on the table, it remains face-up when it's reserved.

Having cards "in hand" doesn't imply whether the cards are public or hidden. For example, in some poker variants player's hand consists of both public and hidden cards.

Also, having less burden of memorising things makes the game more casual and lets players focus on the game itself.

So, I'm really surprised to see most people seem to interpret rules in a way that face-up cards are hidden when reserved.

This *completely* alters the strategy and prevents players from blocking someone from eventually purchasing their reserved cards. It's a huge advantage to players who bank on reservations as a strategy. I've always taught new players that face up reservations must remain face up, and will continue to do so unless a definitive rule is published saying otherwise.
Yes, and in my opinion this is one of MANY problems with that wretched app. Like the even worse Pandemic app, it does not follow its own printed rules.

"The reserved cards are kept in hand and cannot be discarded." Your "hand" in card games is typically private.

And it's clear that reserve cards taken from the deck are hidden, so I'd expect that IF other reserve cards worked differently, THEN there would be some mention of that. So to me, the most obvious conclusion is that cards "in hand" are private. (And so when you take face-up cards into your hand, that his the always-debated-at-BGG Hidden but Trackable Info.)

But indeed I agree that it's not explicitly stated in the rules. Oh well, unclear rules happen. In such a case, I'd go with tradition (your "hand" is secret unless specified otherwise is the usual convention) as well as the fact that multiple software implementations make them hidden, and it seems unlikely they'd all get such a significant thing wrong without the publisher correcting them.

The big problem with the App versions is that you can't even see that the opponents *have* cards in reserve at all. They might have none, they might have three! Who knows?

It seems to be a glaring oversight on the software side.

chunks.embed_text: In the board game Splendor, can a player hide a reserved card from other players, or must they reveal the card before taking it?
chunks.content: The community is divided on whether reserved cards should be hidden or revealed. Some argue that hiding the card is within the rules and adds a memory element to the game, while others believe that revealing the card is more sporting and allows for counter-strategies. The official rules do not explicitly state whether reserved cards should be hidden or revealed, and different players have different interpretations.

raw_thread.body:
This has already come up in an earlier thread, but I wanted to explore this a bit more.

In a four-player game, my daughter's style of play is to reserve high-value cards very early on and focus exclusively on acquiring them.  In our last game, for example, she only purchased five cards, most of which were worth 4-5 points.  

Obviously that takes a lot of gems, and large collections of a single type of gem.  If the rest of the group knew what she was trying to collect, we might try to buy up the extras of that gem before she can get what she needs.  

So on her turn, to prevent that, she slaps her hand over a card to hide it, announces she's reserving it, and pulls it away before anyone can see what its value is, or what gems are required to buy it.  

What's the best way to handle this?

1) Make no changes - it's up to the rest of the group to try to remember the card that is reserved and what she needs in order to buy it.  If you don't remember what it was, tough beans.

2) Leave reserved cards hidden, but only after clearly showing everyone what card is being reserved before taking it and putting it in your hand.

3) Play with reserved cards face up and visible (which counters the opinion on the other thread.)

Maybe we're just lousy players  but we're not doing well countering her strategy.  The rest of us work on building up gem bonuses from the level-1 and level-2 cards, but her strategy is getting her to 15 without any of that.

I would go with your option 2. Showing the card to be reserved before putting it in your hand (hidden) seems sporting to me. Announcing "I am going to reserve this card." while pointing to the card about to be plucked for a few beats should be sufficient.

Huh, just checked since I have the rulebook out from quoting it for your other question.

Turns out it never says anything one way or another about whether reserved cards are hidden information.  It merely says they are kept "in hand."  We've always interpreted that as hidden, because it's slightly more interesting that way to add a memory element to the game and because in most games (except something like Hanabi) "in hand" tends to also mean "hidden."  But choosing whether they're hidden or not appears to be up to house rule.

I'd have to say that option 2 is about the best option to go with. Typically I'm playing the game with just my wife & I so it's different for us with the limited gems. If you're always playing with 4 players I'm surprised and amazed that she'd be able to get all those resources with just so few cards. 

I wouldn't put in the rule to have the reserved cards face up but I would definitely make sure they are kept on the table so that you can see how many a player has. If memory serves me, there is a limit of 3 cards in your hand so I'd just make sure that rule is being enforced.

If it isn't against the rules, it isn't against the rules. When it's her turn, she can slap her hand on the table all she wants. It sounds kind of annoying, but it's not like it gives her an unfair advantage over normal people who just pick the card up and put it in their hand. Bottom line is no one is going to remember what was on it anyway.

Easy solution: take photos of every card that comes out.

Tell her to reserve cards from the draw deck if she wants to play like that.

Just my vote/opinion...

Don't do anything.  She's playing within the rules, and isn't required to (and shouldn't) show you what's on her reserved cards.

If you can't beat her strategy, start using it too.  If she still wins with everyone using that strat, take your game and go home...

Nothing wrong with keeping it secret, but I'd say option 2 is best and fair.

"I'm going to reserve this card" and then pick it up.

Since I'm the polite type of chap when I play Splendor I announce everything I'm doing, pointing to what I have on my tableau and chips when and announcing it when I take something.

However, I don't expect that from anyone I play with and if they choose to just grab stuff without saying anything I accept that and trust what they are doing (I'm also naive) and it's up to me to be keeping track of what they have.

For reserves I have always assumed "in hand" means hidden information in its own way since it is presumed I am paying attention and watching what a player picks up for a reserve.

One note: if she helpfully fills in a space with a card, she is not allowed to look at it first privately, then claim it if it is now her turn.  The card must go on the table for all to see during the previous player's turn and before she hides the information on the card with a slap.
Thanks, all!  We'll think about maybe a house rule for #2 - maybe try it out and see if it's too unfair or not.  I've progressed to a discussion of the strategy itself here.

Of course I don't know the situation, but could this be a good time to mention the idea of sportsmanship? If you insist on taking an advantage that the other players are not themselves taking, legal or no, that might bug some people.

Please don't take offense; I'm sure your daughter's a good egg - just saying kids in general often need a bit of eye-opening where sportsmanship is concerned.

I don't know. Something to think about if you haven't, which you almost certainly have...
We play with #2. The card being reserved is clearly public information at the time. If people forget the card later on that's another issue.

I strongly encourage you to choose option 1, or as well stated above

Don't house rule because you can't beat her strategy, and in my opinion she is definitely well within the spirit of the game. This slapping may be annoying, but it seems like it's effective in psyching you out. Go girl!

Under "Reserve a development card" in the rule book it states:Splendor development from the middle of the table or (if you're feeling

lucky) draw the first card from one of the three decks without showing it to the other players.

So, would you require her to allow you to inspect a drawn card? Also, this last statement implies that the hand is meant to be hidden. So, although less likely and risky, she could beat you without you ever knowing what she's going for until she's completed the card.

My eleven year old son started doing the same thing and we shortly after started routinely beating him through watching what he's trying to collect and draining the supply. We didn't use memory (because our memories are pretty much gone).

Nearly all of the high value cards that require less than 10 gems require several of one color of chip. This strategy she is using requires that she pretty much focus on one color chip, so it should be pretty obvious what she is going for. She will then waste several turns waiting for that color. She will then have to adjust her strategy.

Some thoughts...

1] This thread belongs in "Strategy", not in "Rules". There is no question of rules here.

2] There are only four high-value cards at a time, and each has only one color it really depends on. I don't think it's asking much to look at the four cards and note what colors are needed.

3] You could also look at which chips she's hoarding. That should give you a clue to what she needs.

4] Sportsmanship is a two sided thing. Sure, it's tough on you if she want's to play that way, but it's legal. On the other hand, it's tough on her if you don't feel like playing with her in the future because you don't like the way she plays. That's what sportsmanship is about, putting winning second to making sure everyone is having a good time playing.

4] Sportsmanship also includes you being a good sport about losing. 

I hope this is helpful.

She's playing within the rules. It is unsportsman like to alter the rules. If you want to divert her type of play, simply sit to her right. Then just before your turn is over, announce what each of the point cards require (just before she can take one).

To reserve a card, a player simply needs to take a face-up development from the middle of the table or (if you're feeling lucky) draw the first card from one of the three decks without showing it to the other players.

So, would you require her to allow you to inspect a drawn card?

As I understand the OP, the poster's daughter was reserving cards from table (not from one of the three decks) and not allowing others to see which card she reserved.

It does seem that, if all players always remembered the 12 cards on display, they wouldn't suffer when this kind of thing happens.

But not everyone can do that.  I feel like trying to remember what cards other plays have reserved from the display are part of the game here.  But I'm not sure whether trying to memorize the cards in the display before players reserve (so that you will know which they took if they took one very quickly) is.

It is part of the game if you make it part of the game. It comes down to play style. This then becomes a matter of what kind of people you want to play with.

I knew a guy who played Settlers never making a trade unless it clearly benefited him more than anyone else. Most other people I played with were happy to make trades which seemed to benefit both parties equally.

I know people who play 4X games trying not to get into conflicts and people who look for conflicts wherever they can.

Different play styles.

There's a concept in Go wherein when you can capture an opponent's group of pieces on your next move you announce this to your opponent. It's not mandatory. The idea, I think, is you want to win because you made better decisions than your opponent, not because he missed something. I play every game like this. I remind people of rules or choices they might have forgotten, I make sure they know what I did, etc. I'm not trying to win by tricking someone. I want to win because I made better decisions. But that is my play style.

Under "Reserve a development card" in the rule book it states:Splendor So, would you require her to allow you to inspect a drawn card?

As I understand the OP, the poster's daughter was reserving cards from table (not from one of the three decks) and not allowing others to see which card she reserved.

Yes, sorry for the confusion. I understood the OP's situation. My point was that if she has the option to take a card off of the deck without anyone having any opportunity to see it then it seems fair (at least to me) for her to be able to grab a card that is already face up and fully available for other players to look at without making sure everyone has additional time to look at it after she decides to take it.

Common courtesy - you can hide any card after it is reserved, but any player should be able to review exactly what any other player does on that other player's turn. If she reserves a card from the board, everyone should be able to review THAT TURN what card she is reserving. When the next player acts, it is too late.

Also: demonstrate playing nicely. Show each other what you're doing, in front of her. If Player A asks what daughter did, players B and C should simply say what daughter did, politely and efficiently. There are no hidden actions in Splendor. ("Reserving a card from the top of a deck" is not a hidden action, although the card you actually get that way is hidden.)

How long to you give other players to memorize the card you are going to reserve? Do you point to it and say, "I'm going to reserve that card" and then count to 10 or something before taking it? Giving other players enough time to memorize it gives the other players an advantage. Is that fair to the person reserving it? 

I think that if a person reaches for a card in a normal manner and says "I'm reserving this card" and takes it immediately is fair enough. Reserving a 3rd level card isn't the most efficient action a player can take because they are either giving up buying a card or getting a number of gems to buy a future card. A person isn't guaranteed to be able to complete the reserved card, so that is a wasted inefficient action in hindsight. There is already a risk being taken, so why does someone have to make that even more of a risk by letting the other players memorize that card before you take it?

I agree that common courtesy should prevail, but in Splendor all of the cards up for grabs aside from the draw deck are available for viewing at all times. The player before the player grabbing the cards can stall the ending of his turn so that all players can look at the cards.

If a player has been temporarily distracted then I would agree that perhaps they should have some opportunity to know what just happened, but if this isn't the case then I'd wonder what other players are doing such that they are so caught off guard and can't protect against it within the confines of the rules. If the player is jumping the gun (playing before the previous player has ended their turn) then this is another issue and I'd agree that this should not be allowed.

There are only so many cards that this strategy can work for, those that require 10 gems or less and nearly all of those require large amounts of a single gem color. If the other players are observant they should notice and can take enough of that chip color to disallow the player from amassing that one color. That player will be often taking two of a single color which also should alert the other players. After they have reserved their three cards then they are stuck and can't even get another yellow if their color runs out.

Also, in another thread the player in question is the OP's 10 year old daughter that seems to be outsmarting and psyching out the rest of the players with this strategy. My 11 year old used to do it (not grabbing in this way, but just taking), but after we started keeping an eye out for this sort of play this strategy has failed miserably. My memory is shot, too, so I wouldn't have been able to remember the cards he collected if I had an hour to stare at them. We just watched for the chips he was going for and knew he'd probably want more. Now that we've countered his play I've told him to think of how to tweak his play to see if he can think of another out of the box way of winning that we weren't considering.

Splendor is fairly interactive in that you are all going for the same short supply of cards and chips. You have to play not only for yourself, but directly against what your opponents are doing.

Personally, I find what she's doing to be very unsportsmanlike, and I do think being able to check out what a player's done on their turn is necessary.  I think that any time before the next player acts, anyone should be able to look at the card someone just reserved (assuming it was face up to begin with, of course).  This isn't as much of an issue if the player is being open about what they're taking, but with someone who appears to be deliberately hiding information that should be (briefly) public, I'd say other measures are warranted.

I can't believe so many people are saying that this as acceptable. It is one thing if someone reserves a face-up card and you forget what it was later on, but it is definitely unsportsmanlike to go out of your way to physically prevent another from seeing the card in the first place. Trying to block an opponent from easily purchasing a reserved card is a huge part of the strategy of this game. Also, allowing everyone to see a face-up reserve initially helps give a little edge to doing a blind reserve from the top of a deck instead, because your opponents don't get to see it then.

Do I think players should be able to slap their hand over a card and reserve it? No. 

Do I think other players should get 30 seconds to study a card and recalculate their plans, after someone points to the card they are going to reserve? No.
I'm not seeing too much of a difference between slapping her hand over it or just picking it up very, very quickly

While I can't speak to your daughter's strategy of quickly hiding cards that she reserves, I went to Space Cowboys for clear guidance on this, even though the written rules aren't clear in my mind.

Here's what I got:

Hi Robert,

Reserving a card means taking it for yourself in a way that prevents other people from:

- Seeing it afterwards.

- Confusing it with the cards you already acquired.

It must also be very easy to keep track of the number of cards you have in reserve, and their level.

Hence, yes, keep the cards as if you had them in hand. For all purposes, they work in this way.

Kind regards

François Doucet

The way that I usually play games is to be courteous - I'm taking this card, oh you didn't see it, here take a look.  In all honesty though, this is a game, and if someone isn't paying attention to the game, then that's on them.  Again, I typically play very casually and would rather lose a game in order to keep it entertaining for everyone so they want to play again, however nothing frustrates me more than when you play with someone and it gets around to them and then they say something like "Okay, wait, now what did you guys do?"

Putting the 3 reserved cards sideways does avoid those problems.

For what it's worth, the splendor app handles it this way:

Reserved cards are taken quickly. They don't linger on the screen, and there is no pause for you to inspect or formally acknowledge that the card was taken. This is basically like a player reaching and quickly taking a card off the table while keeping it visible untill it is in their hand. All reserved cards are hidden.

An alternate to this could be to require the player to briefly show the card to all players when taking it. The app doesn't instantly hide a card when a player reserves it, but it's also pretty clear that other players don't get a good long look at it before it goes away either.

In terms of the rules themselves, it is clear that reserved cards are hidden. This comes from the concept of a "hand" universally being hidden information unless otherwise specified. Combined with the rule that if you blind reserve a card from the top of a deck you do not need to show it to other players before putting it in your hand, things become pretty clear. There would be no point in hiding your blind reservation if your hand was not also hidden.

I often play this way since technically, if you are paying close attention and have a decent memory, you should be able to know what each player has reserved anyway. Playing with an open hand just eliminates the need to memorize which I don't find to be particularly fun.

chunks.thread_subject: Taking 3 chips when there only two left
chunks.embed_text: In Splendor, can a player take fewer than three gem tokens if there are not enough available to take three of different colors?
chunks.content: The community resolved that if there are not enough tokens available to take three of different colors, a player may take two tokens or even one. Some users referenced specific rulebook editions that explicitly state this rule, while others inferred it from the game's mechanics.

raw_thread.body:
I noticed this while watching a video about Splendor on the app.

There were only two gem tokens left, let's say one green and one blue. The player proceeded to take the two chips and pass.

How is that even a legal move, when you have to take 3 chips? 

EDIT: Quoting the rules here

- Take 3 gem tokens of different colors.

Nowhere does it say up to 3 gem tokens.

I don't recall where it's stated, but you can definitely take less, but ONLY if there are not 3 available.

Always played it this way with friends and I assumed that the app allows it meant we were right to do so.

Always played it this way with friends and I assumed that the app allows it meant we were right to do so.

Could it be a bug in the app?

Is there an FAQ for this game?

If you read the next paragraph, it says, that if you can't take three chips, then you're allowed to take less (as many as you can).

So: If you can take three chips, you have to. Can't take less. If there are not enough chips, you take as many as you can.

(Edit: Can't quote the rules since I own the german version)

I can't see that in the English version at all. What are you talking about?

EDIT: The only thing I could find is this link

http://www.spacecowboys.fr/faq

I cannot take three tokens. And the two remaining stacks are less than four tokens high. Can I take tokens anyway ?

The action "take three different tokens" allows you to pick only two different tokens, or even one.

So I would say that the wording should be changed to: you may take up to three gem chips.

taking 1 token each of 3 different colors, or from as many colors as are present if there are fewer than 3 different colors in the supply, is always an option. this is in contrast to the other 3 options, which are each conditional (you may only take two tokens of the same color if at least two tokens of that color remain afterwards; you may only reserve a card if you currently have fewer than 3 reserved cards; you may only purchase a card if you have the required tokens and discounts from previously purchased cards to pay the price.) it is theoretically possible that you can do nothing on your turn, if all tokens have been taken by players, you have the full hand of 3 reserved cards, and you are unable to buy any cards from your hand or in the tableau, but i have never seen this happen.

for example, if there are only 4 white and 2 blue chips in the supply, you are allowed to take 1 white and 1 blue (you are not forced to take 2 white, nor are you prevented from taking 1 of each when there are fewer than 3 colors available).

The rule has *always* been that, in order to take two gems of the same color, there needs to be four tokens of that color *before* you take gems. Or, if you prefer, there need to be two gems of that color remaining *after* you take gems.

So, in a two player game, a long as the stack is full (has all four gems available), then you can take two of them on your turn.

My copy is from 2014, and that's how that rule has always worked.

chunks.content: The community resolved that players must use their development cards first to cover the cost of a new card, before spending any chip tokens. The rulebook does indeed cover this.
The rulebook does indeed cover this.

It says you *must* use your card discounts first, before spending any chips:

Splendor Rules, Pg. Thus, if a player has 2 blue bonuses and wants to purchase a card which costs 2 blue tokens and 1 green token, the player must only spend 1 green token.

(bold emphasis mine).  It does not say you *may* spend two blue tokens and one green token if you have them, even if you have two blue cards.  It says you *must* only spend the one green token.  And, by association, that also means you *must* apply the two blue discount from your two blue cards.

That said, it's your game.  If you want to house rule it that way, then go ahead.  Just know that your method is indeed a house rule, and is not the official, nor intended, way of play according to the rulebook.

Cheers!

chunks.content: The community resolved that players can choose how to pay for a card, using either gems, cards, or a combination of both. It is recommended to use cards to save gems for future turns. You decide what to do.  I recommend using your 5 emerald cards and saving your 2 emerald gems in order to buy a card requiring 6 or 7 emeralds on a later turn.  Remember:  you expend gems but you keep cards.

Considering that you cannot hold more than 10 gems from the end of one turn to the next, you can always get rid of any emerald gems that you feel are unneeded when you take 2 or 3 new gems from the supply. You can take more gems putting you over 10, but you have to get rid of enough to get back down to 10 before your turn ends.  In other words, let's say that I have 10 gems including those 2 emerald gems.  I take 2 ruby gems from the supply, raising my total to 12.  To get back down to 10, I return the 2 emerald gems to the supply.   That's my turn.

For me, I'd go with keeping the 2 emerald gems and just using the 5 emerald cards.  It's what we call a 'free card' in our Splendor games because you are actually spending nothing.

Relevant rules quote:
The bonuses a player has from development cards acquired
on previous turns provide discounts on the purchase of new
cards. Each bonus of a given color is equal to a token of that
color.
Thus, if a player has 2 blue bonuses and wants to purchase a
card which costs 2 blue tokens and 1 green token, the player
must only spend 1 green token.
If a player has enough development cards (and therefore
bonuses), they can even purchase a card without spending any
tokens.
